steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage describes a study finding a "broad inverse relationship" between a brand's market share and the increase in cross-category purchase probability. The blank must explain WHY high market share correlates with a smaller cross-category boost, and low market share with a larger one.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the purchase probability of a brand is affected by the purchase probability of the particular product category to which it belongs, and thus brands in categories that are rarely purchased tend to have low purchase probabilities regardless of market share.’: Discusses category-level purchase rates, not market share. This doesn't explain why higher market share brands see a smaller cross-category lift. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘as a brand's market share increases, the number of competing brands in the same product category tends to decrease, and thus the rate of increase in market share tends to accelerate as a brand's market share grows.’: Addresses competitive dynamics within a single category, not the cross-category purchase probability relationship the study measured. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘brands with high market share have high purchase probability regardless of whether the other product in the pair is purchased and thus have less potential to increase in purchase probability than brands with low market share do.’: This is a ceiling effect. High-share brands are already purchased frequently, so being purchased in a second category adds little additional probability. Low-share brands have room to grow. This directly explains the inverse relationship. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘consumers tend to be less familiar with brands with low market share than brands with high market share and thus may purchase both products in a pair with low market share without recognizing that the products are the same brand.’: This would actually suppress the cross-category effect for low-share brands, the opposite of what the study found. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
